titleStmt.title: Napoli, Biblioteca Nazionale, MS XIII.B.29. Edizione diplomatica e interpretativa

objectDesc.p: Codice pergamenaceo, 146 fogli. Dettagli bibliografici in Guddatt-Figge, G., Catalogue of Manuscripts containing Middle English Romances, Munich, 1976.
Una sola mano, stile secretariale. Mani successive visibili nelle note e nei disegni.

listBibl.bibl: O. Khalaf, R. Cioffi, "Un progetto di trascrizione ed edizione digitale delcodice Napoli, Biblioteca Nazionale, MS XIII.B.29", Umanistica Digitale 2 (2018), 139-159. Url: https://umanisticadigitale.unibo.it/article/view/7262

editorialDecl.p: Facsimile ed edizione interpretativa, dove si è provveduto alla normalizzazione grafematica, alla correzione degli errori scribali e all'aggiunta della punteggiatura secondo gli standard moderni.

div.p: Metro Tail-rhyme stanzas di 6 versi dal v. 1 al 474. Il resto del poema è costruito in rima baciata

Trama (in inglese)
The elderly Earl of Southampton and his young wife have a son, Bevis. When he is seven, Bevis’ mother has the Earl murdered by her lover, the Emperor of Almayn. Bevis’ uncle Saber protects him, but after he beats his stepfather his mother sells him to Saracen merchants. The child is taken to King Ermin of Armenia, who encourages him to renounce Christianity. He refuses, but trains as a knight until, at the age of fifteen, his companions insult his faith and he kills them all. Ermin’s daughter Josian calms her father’s rage and heals Bevis’ wounds. Her love for him grows as he slays a monstrous boar and fights off an ambush by Ermin’s jealous steward. King Brademond demands to marry Josian, threatening war if he is refused. Ermin knights Bevis and Josian gives him a war-horse called Arundel. He defeats Brademond, but releases him after he agrees to pay tribute. During the celebrations, Josian declares her love for Bevis, but is rejected until she promises to become Christian. A treacherous chamberlain tells Ermin, and the King sends Bevis to Brademond with a letter demanding his own death. He is thrown into a dungeon and Ermin marries his daughter to King Yvor of Mombraunt. Josian, however, uses a magic ring to protect her chastity. After seven years, Bevis escapes from prison. He travels to Mombraunt and, disguised as a pilgrim, rescues Arundel and Josian, who assures him that she is still a virgin. As they escape, he makes Ascopart, King Yvor’s giant, his page. They sail to Cologne, where Josian is baptised and Ascopart rejects Christianity. Bevis then saves the city from a poisonous dragon before travelling to England, where he insults his stepfather and is reunited with Saber. While he is away, Josian is forced to marry another Earl, but murders him on their wedding night. Bevis arrives just in time to save her from being burnt, and they return to England, where Saber and the Emperor of Almayn are preparing for battle. Bevis leads his troops to victory, and Ascopart captures the Emperor, who is killed in a cauldron of pitch. Bevis’ mother dies and he is recognised as Lord of Hampshire. He marries Josian and travels to London, where King Edgar makes him his marshal. When the king’s son is killed trying to steal Arundel, however, Bevis and his pregnant wife leave England, taking Saber's son Terri as their page. The rejected Ascopart betrays Bevis by offering to return Josian to Yvor. He kidnaps her just after she has given birth to twin sons, Miles and Guy, but she keeps herself pure by pretending to be leprous. Bevis and Terri leave the children with foster parents and ride on to Aumbeforce, where Bevis wins a tournament and the heart of the princess, though she agrees to marry Terri if Josian returns. Meanwhile Saber dreams of Bevis’ troubles and sets out to find him. He slays Ascopart and rescues Josian, who supports their search by working as a minstrel. After seven years, they reach Aumbeforce, where the couple and their children are reunited, and Terri marries the princess. They all travel to Armenia and find Yvor besieging King Ermin. Bevis captures Yvor, but releases him when he agrees to pay ransom. Ermin dies, leaving Guy his heir, and he and Bevis convert their subjects to Christianity. Yvor returns and steals Arundel, vowing revenge against Bevis, but Saber retrieves the horse and rides to Armenia, followed by Yvor’s troops. Yvor challenges Bevis to single combat and fights bravely, but Bevis is victorious. The rest of the Saracens are defeated, and Bevis is crowned king of Mombraunt. Saber hears that King Edgar has seized his lands, and they all sail to England. Bevis appeals to the king, but his steward accuses the knight of treachery and raises the citizens of London against him. Battle rages in the city’s streets until the steward is dead and the Thames runs red. Edgar offers his daughter and kingdom to Miles, then Bevis gives Hampshire to Saber and returns to Mombrant with Josian and Arundel, leaving Gui in Armenia and Terri in Aumberforce. After twenty years Josian dies, swiftly followed by Arundel and Bevis. They are buried in a lavish tomb, and their sons found an abbey to pray for their souls.

Altri testimoni
Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.2.38, fol. 102v-134r
Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 175/96, pag. 131-156
Cambridge, Trinity College Library, MS O.2.13, fol. 149r-152r
Edinburgh, National Library f Scotland, MS Advocates 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript), fol. 176r-201r
London, British Library, MS Egerton 2862, fol. 45r-96v
Manchester, Chetham's Library, MS 8009, fol. 122r-190v
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Douce Fragm. E.13
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Eng. Poet. D.208, fol. 49r-50v

div.l: ordingis lystenith to my tale
is merious þan nyȝtingale
hat I wolle yow synge
f a knyght sir Beno
hat was bore in Southampton
with outenwithouten lesyng
e was a alworthe man
nd many kingdomes wan
o god isGodis lawis
e was best come in feld
nd mot wan with pere shild
i his lyfe daies
I woll yowe telle al to gadiraltogadir
f th knyȝt and of his fadir
he good Erle sir Gy
f ampton he was lord ire
nd of alle that ilke shire
him to wardy
LordlingLordinges Erle I of telle
n his tyme man of flehe ne felle
was none so stronge
nd euer he lyvid out wife
s he was in eche trife
ille late and long
Tho he was fallen in elde
hathe ne myȝt him silfehimsilfe welde
he wolde a wife take
nd sone aftirtheraftir I ndurstond
him had ben le þan all lond
had he hur forake.
A wife in elde he toke an hond
he kyngis doghte of Skotlond
o feire and bright
las that he hu e chese
is owne life for hur he lese
with mochell nright
This maide I haue of tolde
faire woman shoshe was and bolde
nd free I boreibore
Of Almayne the mou
hur louyd amour
welle longe ther bi forebifore
Oft to hur fadir he sent
nd hym silfehymsilfethedir went
for hur sake
moche he desirid hur to wyue
The kyng fo no thing on lyue
wolde hur him take
And sithen he yaue hur to sir Gy
Astalewortha staleworth man and an hardy
f Southampto
But whan he fille in tointo elde
ebill and waxen vn weldeunwelde
i right resoun
So long thei yede to gadirtogadir to bedde
man childe to gadirtogadir thei hedde
hat Beuys hate
A faire childe he was and bolde
e was but vijseuen yere olde
ho his fadir him lete
he lady al a mysamys hu though
nd moche a yenayen right ho wrought
n hir tolkre
y lorde is olde and may not wirch
l day him is leuer to be at chirch
hen in my bowre
ad I take a yong knyght
hat had ne be bruid in fyght
s he nowe is
he wolde me loue with all his myȝt
lip and kys bothe day and nyȝt
nd make me blys
I nelle lette for no thinge
hat I nelle to dethe him bryng
ith sum braide
Anone right that lady fre
lepid to counel hur meyngere
nd to him saide
eingere do me sewerte
hat thou alt not dicouery me
neuer to noo wight
nd yef thow wolt that hit so bee
I wolde the yeue bothe golde and fee
nd make the knyght
Than answerid the mesynger
ffals he was and pautener
nd well prowte
adame but I do well thi nede
I wolle that thowe me for bedeforbede
hi londe throwout
The ladi ther oftherof was fulle fayne
ynde sho seide to Almayne
ut of the towre
esynger be queynt and snelle
nd on my halfe grete him welle
hat mour
nd bid him that in the first day
hat comyth in the monthe of ay
ffor the loue of mee
hat he be with his men prest
ffor to fyght in one forest
ppon the ee
y lord I wol thedir sende
ffor thi loue to shende
With fewe mayne
id him that it nought be leued
hat he smyte ofe my lorde islordis hed
nd sende hyt vn tounto me
And whan he hat o y doneydone
my loue he wynnyth to come gone
withoute delay
Then seid the mesynger
adam wol welle one be there
owe haue good day
forthe the mesinger he goth
rist make him wroth
hat him wrought
o ship the meinger yode
Alas the wynde was to good
nd him ouer broght
Whan he come in tointo Almayne
ho he mette with a wayne
nd dir grete him welle
Sir he seid amour
hare hall I fynd emour
Thou me telle
I wol the telle a noneanone right
t Rapain he lay to nyghttonyght
i my swere
The mesynger him thonkid a nonanon
nd to Rapayn he gan gon
With outwithout dynere
The emour ther he found
nd downe he knelid on ground
s hit was right
Sir the lady of south hampton/>Southamptoun
he grettith well bi maryMary's son
hat is so bright
nd biddith yow in the first day
hat comyth in the monthe of ay
howe so it be
hat ye be with your men t
for to fight in hur forest
Bi sidistBisidist the see
ur lord sho woll thedir ynd
ffor your loue for to shynd
itw fewe maynee
nd ther a bouttherabout ho hal be bey
nd ye hur halt wed powy
o your mee
So sey thy lady that hur hest
yef that my life may let
hit halle be doo
I am gladdir I the say
han eny tong tel may
And sey hur soo
ffor thou woldist hur erand lede
n hors chargid with gold rede
I woll the yeue
And with inwithin this furtenyght
my silfemysilfe I wolle dubbe knyght
yef that I leue
The meynger him þonkind yorne
home a gayneagayne he gan turne
o South hamptonSouthampton
The lady lay on hur tour
nd aftir him sent to hur bour
nd gan to owne
addam he seid I the telle
he mour the gretith welle
ith loue met
Glad he was of that tithing
nd wol be pret at fiȝtyng
n that foret
ef thou art glad my lord to sle
laddir he is for loue of the
elle fele sithe
The meinger that this eid

div.p:
Trama (in inglese)
Sir Isumbras is a rich and courteous knight, with a beautiful wife and three sons, who has forgotten that he owes his good fortune to God. One day, a bird sent by Christ reminds him of his sin and offers him a choice between suffering in youth or old age. Isumbras repents, and promises to serve Christ while he is young and strong. He calmly accepts his fate as his horse dies, his hawks and hounds flee and his property burns down. He and his family leave the county and wander where Christ leads them, begging for food. As they are crossing a river, Isumbras’ two eldest sons are carried away by a lion and a leopard. Isumbras urges his wife and youngest son to accept God’s will, and the three continue to the Greek sea, where they find the war ships of a Saracen king. Isumbras refuses to convert in return for food and rejects the king’s offer to purchase his wife. She is forcibly taken from him, and he is left, badly beaten, with a cloth full of gold. Before the ships sail the pair is granted a final meeting, where she gives him half of her ring and tells him to search for her. Distraught, Isumbras continues on his way, until a griffin steals his gold and his youngest son is taken by a unicorn. Left alone, he submits himself to God’s will. He joins a community of ironsmiths and spends seven years carrying ore and learning to make armour. When he hears that the Saracens are waging war against a Christian ruler, Isumbras rides a work horse into battle and defeats them, killing their king. The Christian king thanks the mysterious knight and takes him to a nunnery. As soon as his wounds are healed, however, Isumbras dresses as a pilgrim, and continues to wander. He sails to the Holy Land and spends seven years living in poverty until another angel informs him that his sins are forgiven. He then travels to the court of a charitable queen, but weeps instead of eating the feast that is laid before him. Full of pity, the queen gives him a room in her castle, where he regains his strength and beats her best knights in a tournament. One day Isumbras finds a nest containing his cloth and gold, which he conceals under his bed. When the jealous Saracens discover it, they show it to the queen, who realises that it belonged to her husband. Isumbras tells his story and shows her his ring. She produces her half, and the couple are reunited. Isumbras is crowned and decrees the kingdom Christian, but his men desert him when he is attacked by the Saracens. As he and his wife prepare to fight, they are joined by three knights, riding on a leopard, a lion and a unicorn. The five of them defeat the Saracen host, and the knights reveal that they are Isumbras’ sons, sent back to him by God. The family rejoices, and conquers the lands of another three Saracen kings, crowning Isumbras’ sons and converting their inhabitants. When they die, their souls all go to heaven.

Altri testimoni
Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 175, fol. 98r–107r
Edinburgh, National Library of Scotland, MS Advocates 19.3.1, fol. 48r–56v
Lincoln Cathedral Library, MS 91 ('Thornton Manuscript'), fol. 109r–114v
London, British Library, MS Cotton Caligula A.ii, fol. 130r–134r
London, Gray's Inn, MS 20, fol. 228r228-v
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 61, fol. 9r–16r
Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Douce 261, fol. 1r–7v
Oxford, University College, MS 142, fol. 128r

div.l: e made be hevyn erthe
al worlde in daies seuyn
that is ful of myȝt
send s alle his bleyng
las and more olde and yong
and kepe s day and nyȝt
wol you telle of a knyȝt
that was douȝty in ilke a fiȝt
in towne and eke in fielde
ther durt no man his dynt a bideabide
with pere ne with childe
an he was cuthe y noweynowe
x to drawe in his plowe
and tedis in his talle
man he was curteye and kynde
euery man was his frende
he was lord of alle
Curteis and kynde he was
his name was clepid ir Iombrase
othe curteis and fre
the gretti of his curtey
ne couthe ther no man ditry
fulle good man was he
Eke men he louid in halle
and ȝaue ham obus of riche palle
bothe golde and eke fe
f curteie he was kyng
his curtefie had no endyng
n worlde is none so fre
s feire a wife had he
as eny in cuthe miȝt be
as telle it ȝowe
and knowe childryn had he thre
as feire as eny myȝt be
for they were feire y noweynowe
Into his hert a pride was brouȝt
of god isGodis worke he had no rouȝt
his merci for to nomme
So longe he vayned in hiȝe de
that od uolde no lengue a bideabide
but end to him a tewyne
So hit bi fillebifille ppon a day
the knyȝt went furthe him to play
his forest for to e
and as he went in a narowe way
a fowle he hird him to say
that sate ppon a tre
and aid elcome Iumbras
hou hat for ȝeteforȝete whate thou was
for pride and golde an fe
he kynge of heuyn wel gretith the
in ȝouthe o elde thou getit pouerte
nd chee whethir thou haddist le tobe to be
ith careful hert and sikyng seresore
he fille ppon his kneys there
and his hondis p he hilde
orldis welthe wol for sakeforsake
and to ristis merci me take
myne hert to hym ȝilde
n yowthe may ride and go
in lde may nouȝt do so
my bonis wol wax olde
ord ȝif thi wille be
yn youthe send hit me
and welthe in myne elde
wey the fowle takith his fliȝt
and al a lonealone leuys the knyȝt
ful sone he went his way
nd whan he had the fowle for loreforlore
his stede that was so liȝt bi forebifore
undir him gan die
is houndis and his hawkis bothe
wenten to wode to him were lothe
ilke one to dyuers way
hat wondir was þan þei he were wo
n fote him heuith for to go
to payne turnyth alle his play
nd also by the wode he went
a litil knaue was to him sent
that come hym a ȝayneaȝayne
he come to tal him a won tithing
that brende walwas al his thing
and his bestis slayne
ord ther nys nouȝt left on lyue
but ȝowre childryn and ȝoure wyue
i that me bouȝt
he seid ȝif thei may on lyue be
mi wife and my childryn iijthree
alle drede me riȝt nouȝt
nd alle he went silue alone
his hurde men he met ilke one
he seidhate eylith yowe
ey seid fee was from hem revid
ord no thingnothing is yowe bi leuydbileuyd
an ox to youre plowe
hey wept alle and did hem ille
the knyȝt bade ham alle be stille
ne wite yow this wo
od ȝewith s and od take
and at is wille riche e make
and pore men also
deleful siȝt he gan ther se
his wife and his children thre
out of the fyre were fled
as nakid as they as thei were borne
they sittith stille ndur a thorne
brent out of hare bed
e chaungid he no thingnothing of his ble
tille he sawe ham nakid be
that ere were comely clad
he lady bade ham be blithe
your fadre come ful swithe
for no thinge be ye a dradadrad
hey wepte alle and made gret care
the knyȝt bade ham leue har fare
and wepe not so sore
lle that care that we be yn
it is for our wikke syn
orthe we were wel more
and we al come no work wirch
our frend of vus schulle be
ouer londe rede we fare

div.p: Metro breve poema a rima alternata
Breve poema in italiano sull'imperatore Costantino e su sua madre, Sant'Elena, la scopritrice della Vera Croce.

lg.l: con mia matre Elena Ininmeſtauocemesta uoce
tro waytroway del mio factore quellacrocequella croce
qual tene tucto el mundo Jnsuoin suo domino
